metrics.narrative: Amy Freeman articulated clear qualitative pain but no quantified value case. On the intro call (2026-06-17) she described manual, error-prone intake: 'we have so much room for automation' and detailed that suppliers ignore the Coupa portal so 'we're just having them old school send in forms to us, and then we have to key those in.' She also wants project-spend roll-up for corporate property build-outs (the Charlotte building example). No cost, headcount, cycle-time, or savings figure was ever stated, so the business impact is unquantified. Quantifying the cost of manual keying and failed supplier onboarding is the next move.

champion.narrative: Amy Freeman is a developing coach, not yet a confirmed champion. She is genuinely enthusiastic ('This has been very enlightening. We didn't even know we had this option') and volunteered to carry the materials to her manager ('Wait to present it to my manager and see what her thoughts are'), which is real advocacy. What is missing is power and access: she has no budget authority and her sponsor is a brand-new interim manager she is still feeding context to. To become a true champion she needs to secure a meeting with that manager (and ideally a VP-level sponsor) and push the initiative internally, not just relay a deck.

competition.narrative: The competitive frame is incumbent-displacement plus do-nothing. First Horizon is a full-suite Coupa shop (P2P, invoices, expenses, CLM, analytics, on Oracle ERP) and Coupa is steering them to its newly acquired intake/orchestration product (Tonkean, which Amy recalled from Coupa Inspire in May 2026): 'they're recommending that we work with the relevant stakeholder wedge is the weakness Amy herself voiced in Coupa's intake/onboarding: 'Coupa basically told us that we have to onboard our own suppliers, and they didn't really provide much support,' versus the Teams-native, low-IT Merlin intake she preferred. The realistic battle is Merlin Intake bolted onto Coupa vs Coupa+Tonkean, and against the status quo of manual processes.

identify_pain.narrative: The operational pain is clearly confirmed and owned by procurement operations. Amy described a broken intake/orchestration process and a failed Coupa supplier-onboarding (SIM) rollout: 'We have been very unsuccessful with SIEM... we've turned off all the automation... and we're just having them old school send in forms to us.' Suppliers ignore the Coupa portal ('They think it's spam. They ignore it') and most email invoices without PO references, forcing manual keying. She also flagged a cloud/AI-adoption hurdle ('we don't use any AI. We're trying to get that pushed across the finish line') tied to a prior cloud-resistant CIO. The pain owner is Amy and her two-person Coupa admin team.

paper_process.narrative: No contracting or paper mechanics have been discussed. There is no mention of MSA/SOW, legal, procurement channel, or InfoSec on the 2026-06-17 call. For a regulated ~$100B bank, InfoSec and IT review are predictable gates, and any deployment would be a bolt-on to the incumbent Coupa suite and Oracle ERP, so integration scoping will be required. None of this is yet visible; a discovery question on procurement/security process and the Coupa contract terms would create visibility.

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is identified or engaged. The only contact, Amy Freeman, is the senior Coupa administrator in procurement operations (29 years at the bank, 13 in procurement) and explicitly lacks budget authority for a $120k deal. She told the rep her direct manager left two weeks before the 2026-06-17 call and an interim manager 'has only been here a year' on Coupa now owns the area; that interim manager is the immediate gate but is unnamed and unengaged. For a $100B+ bank, expect VP Procurement or CIO sign-off, and the prefetched account roster shows candidates (e.g. a Sr. VP Manager Strategic Sourcing, a Deputy CIO), but none are in this deal. Identifying and reaching the budget owner is the rank-1 gap to close.

decision_process.narrative: The buying process is unknown beyond the first internal step. On the 2026-06-17 call the only process Amy described was to take the recording and deck to her interim manager: 'Is there any way that I could have this recording to share with my manager?' and 'Wait to present it to my manager and see what her thoughts are.' Beyond that manager review there is no defined approval chain, no procurement or IT process, no RFP, and no decision timeline. The call attendance itself is a single thread (rep plus Amy), which signals the process has not yet opened beyond one operator. Mapping the approval path is required to advance.

decision_criteria.narrative: Amy named concrete evaluation criteria on the 2026-06-17 call: minimal IT effort ('Minimal I p IT dependency... If you on a skeleton IT crew'), low/no-code configuration ('plain chat words, zero training, or change management'), a conversational Teams-native intake experience (she praised 'using it in Teams... There's no training in that'), and an all-inclusive project-management tool to roll up project spend across vendors. These are stated preferences, not a formal documented requirements set, and there is no RFP. The criteria are real and favorable to Zycus but not yet validated by other stakeholders.

Build a savings model (time saved, error reduction, adoption lift) to give her a quantified case to present to management and the budget owner. This is the wedge that moves the deal from 'interesting' to 'funded.'

Convert today's follow-up into a demo commitment with Amy's manager or the broader team: Amy validated fit conceptually ('This is exactly what we're looking for') but has not yet seen Merlin Intake or iManage in action. She is presenting materials to her manager this week; if the manager is receptive, the next logical step is a live demo to show the Teams-native intake experience and the iManage project roll-up capability (her explicit ask). On today's call (2026-06-26), propose a demo for next week with Amy, her manager, and any IT or procurement stakeholders involved. A demo with the interim manager is the gate to the broader decision process and the Economic Buyer.
